reasoning: First iteration - generating initial query implementations. Learner identified critical optimizations: Q6 needs aggressive Parquet row group pruning (8x I/O reduction), Q3 needs proper join ordering and column projection (81% I/O reduction on lineitem), Q1 needs string key aggregation support. All queries should leverage sorted Parquet data and column projection.

query_guidance.Q1: Implement scan-aggregate pipeline: (1) ParquetScan with date filter l_shipdate <= '1998-09-02' and column projection [l_shipdate, l_returnflag, l_linestatus, l_quantity, l_extendedprice, l_discount, l_tax] - expect to read 784/800 row groups due to high selectivity. (2) Use Arrow compute kernels (arrow::compute::Multiply, Subtract, Add) to vectorize expression evaluation for disc_price=l_extendedprice*(1-l_discount) and charge=disc_price*(1+l_tax). (3) Implement StringKeyHashAggregate or dictionary-encode l_returnflag/l_linestatus to int32 for group-by (expect 4 groups: N/O, N/F, R/F, A/F). (4) Compute aggregates: SUM(l_quantity, l_extendedprice, disc_price, charge), AVG(l_quantity, l_extendedprice, l_discount), COUNT(*). (5) In-memory sort by l_returnflag, l_linestatus (trivial on 4 rows). Critical: string group key support is required for correctness.
query_guidance.Q3: Implement 3-way join pipeline with optimized join order: (1) ParquetScan customer with string filter c_mktsegment='BUILDING' and column projection [c_custkey, c_mktsegment] - expect 300K rows (20% selectivity). (2) ParquetScan orders with date filter o_orderdate < '1995-03-15' and column projection [o_custkey, o_orderkey, o_orderdate, o_shippriority] - use row group pruning to read only 60/150 row groups, expect 6M rows. (3) HashJoin(customer, orders) on c_custkey=o_custkey with customer as BUILD side (smaller: 300K vs 6M), output ~6M rows. (4) ParquetScan lineitem with date filter l_shipdate > '1995-03-15' and column projection [l_orderkey, l_extendedprice, l_discount] - use row group pruning to read only 400/800 row groups (critical: 81% I/O reduction from 3/16 columns), expect 30M rows. (5) HashJoin(customer_orders, lineitem) on o_orderkey=l_orderkey with customer_orders as BUILD side (6M vs 30M), output ~9M rows. (6) Use Arrow compute kernels to evaluate revenue=l_extendedprice*(1-l_discount). (7) MultiKeyHashAggregate on [l_orderkey, o_orderdate, o_shippriority] computing SUM(revenue), expect 100K-500K groups. (8) Top-K sort by revenue DESC, o_orderdate with LIMIT 10 (use heap/partial sort, avoid full sort). Critical: aggressive Parquet pruning on lineitem (read 400/800 row groups), proper join ordering (customer→orders→lineitem), and column projection (3/16 columns on lineitem).
query_guidance.Q6: Implement scan-filter-aggregate pipeline with maximum Parquet pruning: (1) ParquetScan with date range filter l_shipdate >= '1994-01-01' AND l_shipdate < '1995-01-01' and column projection [l_shipdate, l_discount, l_quantity, l_extendedprice] - CRITICAL: leverage sorted l_shipdate to read only 100/800 row groups (12.5% of data, 8x I/O reduction). (2) Apply in-memory filters: l_discount BETWEEN 0.05 AND 0.07 (~10% selectivity), then l_quantity < 24 (~48% selectivity), reducing 7.5M rows to ~360K. Use FilterByRange for numeric comparisons. (3) Use Arrow compute kernels (arrow::compute::Multiply) to evaluate revenue=l_extendedprice*l_discount on ~360K rows. (4) Compute single SUM(revenue) using arrow::compute::Sum or scalar accumulation (no GROUP BY). Critical: Parquet row group pruning is the primary optimization - must read only 100/800 row groups based on min/max statistics of l_shipdate. This is the highest-impact optimization across all queries (87.5% data reduction).
